summary: Stacey Gabriel discusses the importance of adopting the CRAM file format for genomic data. She highlights that standardizing file formats not only reduces storage costs by two-thirds but also prevents the wasteful reprocessing of data, enabling seamless sharing across large-scale research consortiums like the All of Us Research Program.

principles.statement: Genomic data must be standardized into formats that can be easily and effectively shared.
principles.rationale: Standardization prevents the massive waste of time and resources that occurs when researchers have to reformat data before analyzing it, while also drastically reducing storage costs.
principles.source_quote: And it becomes absolutely critical that that data is in a format that can be easily and effectively shared amongst many investigators.

signature_quotes.text: In other words, it really becomes very wasteful if an investigator has to go and reprocess or reformat the data every time they get a new data set in order to perform an analysis.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the necessity of standardized file formats for genomic data sharing.

signature_quotes.text: And it becomes absolutely critical that that data is in a format that can be easily and effectively shared amongst many investigators.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the value of the CRAM file format beyond just storage savings.

anti_patterns.description: Requiring individual investigators to reprocess or reformat data every time they receive a new dataset.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates massive inefficiencies and waste, slowing down analysis and hindering collaboration across large research consortiums.
